date: 2026-06-16
video_id: v_m_5IkxJc0
url: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=v_m_5IkxJc0
title: The Macro Minute: When should investors care about the circular financing aspects of AI?

Happy Tuesday out there, team 42. It's your skipper here, Darius Dale, with our macro minute for Tuesday, June 16th, 2026. As always, we'll start with the executive summary from today's lead-off morning note. So, let's dive right in. Today's key macro question is, when should investors care about the circular financing aspects of AI? The short answer is concerns regarding the circular financing aspects of the AI theme are simply mistimed if global liquidity is expanding. Investors should table these concerns until the global liquidity cycle inflects into a downtrend, which would which would likely be roughly coincident with a phase transition to a risk-off market regime. In terms of analytical nuance, the latest discussion regarding the circular financing aspects of the AI theme comes amid Nvidia selling 25 billion of high-grade bonds. Orders were massively oversubscribed at $85 billion, lifting Nvidia's first sale since 2021 from a $20 billion target. A key AI supplier, Nvidia, resides at the center of the circular financing debate with a $5 billion Intel stake and investments of 10 billion and 30 billion in Anthropic and OpenAI's latest funding rounds. Alphabet bought a stake in SpaceX in 2015, yielding many billions in paper gains on only a $900 million outlay. Alphabet and Amazon hold significant Anthropic exposure, and Microsoft owns 27% of OpenAI, a stake currently worth $228 billion. Anthropic's valuation jump to nearly $1 trillion implies a notable earnings windfall for Nvidia, Alphabet, and Amazon. Elsewhere, in Japan, the Bank of Japan hiked its benchmark policy rate to the highest level in 31 years and signaled further hikes ahead. In China, despite ample scope to continue supplying liquidity from persistent below-trend and well-below-target inflation, the PBOC has shrunk its balance sheet for the second consecutive month on a three-month annualized basis. The current 13% decline represents the sharpest contraction among major central banks. The ECB is down 4%, the Bank of Japan down 11% Swiss National Bank up 1% Bank of England unchanged and the Fed up 5%. The contraction of the PBC's balance sheet is causing Chinese liquidity to decelerate. Still, China's current plus 8% through month annualized growth rate represents the fastest rate of liquidity expansion among the major liquidity supplying economies. The ECB's down 5%, Japan's down 9% 9%, Switzerland's down 3%, the UK's up 2%, and the US is up 7%. The deceleration in the rate of Chinese liquidity expansion is causing global liquidity to slow as well. The current three month annualized growth rate of 3% represents the slowest rate of global liquidity expansion since November of 2025. Worse, key leading indicators of global liquidity currently signal a peaking uptrend over the medium term according to the 42 macro global liquidity model. This implies the current risk on market regime will run out of steam if the Fed chooses to not look through accelerating above target inflation. So, as always, I'll wrap up with a question from our community. This one's titled There are AI stocks in the bonds. Since Bridgewater folks like to say they are bonds in the stocks, uh there are bonds in the stocks. Well, this fascinating Torsten Slok slide from Apollo shows there are now AI stocks uh in the bonds. So, blowing this uh blowing this up here, uh we have uh if you look at uh investment grade issuance year-to-date, according to Apollo, uh uh 49% of that is uh AI. Venture capital, 87% of uh venture capital fundraising is AI year-to-date. And 38% of high yield uh fundraising year-to-date is AI. So, uh this is highly supportive of one of our core research views, which is that the AI capex bubble is likely to conclude in a secular bear market. Um capex bubbles historically here in the US economy have always concluded in secular bear markets. When you cite the railroad capex bubble, the consumer durable goods capex bubble, namely autos uh in the in the 1920s, and then the uh internet technology capex bubble that we saw in the uh 1990s. And so it you know there's a lot of reasons from a from the perspective of the the the the frequentist probability on that, but also from a Bayesian standpoint if you think about the fraying geopolitical world order, the fraying domestic political world order, the likelihood that we see you know significant wealth redistributive policies, trust busting, etc. in the coming years here in the US is all things that are going to contribute to what we believe is likely to be a secular market on the other side of this AI bubble, but if we do pull back and have a correction this summer, investors need to be aware that that's probably not the end of the game. That's probably not the end of the rainbow. There's probably more There is likely to be more upside ahead in the context of our paradigm shift theme into 2027. However, I think when you look into 2028 and beyond that's where you have to get very concerned about a potential secular bear market. So this data point is very much in support of that thesis given that everybody's going to have one position on in their portfolio and when they go to book gains, there'll be nobody on the other side of the trade. So we'll wrap it up there. Terry Sandquist here presenting our macro minute for Tuesday, June 16th, 2026. Best of luck out there today. We'll catch back here tomorrow. Cheers.
